trm:term.trm:name: Drift
trm:term.trm:definition: Divergence between a spec node and its mapped artifact, detected by comparing stored hashes against current content. Spec drift occurs when a node changes after an artifact mapping is created. Artifact drift occurs when code changes without updating the mapping. Both directions are detectable because mappings pin hashes for both sides.

trm:term.trm:name: Artifact Mapping
trm:term.trm:definition: A bidirectional link between a spec node at a specific revision and a code artifact at a specific repository revision. Each side carries a content hash for drift detection. The mapping also declares coverage: full, partial, or none.

trm:term.trm:name: C14N
trm:term.trm:definition: XML Canonicalization, a standard algorithm that produces a deterministic byte-for-byte representation of an XML subtree. The clayers tooling uses C14N with rewrite_prefixes mode so that node hashes are independent of namespace prefix choices. Two documents expressing the same content with different prefixes produce the same canonical form and therefore the same hash.

trm:term.trm:name: Node Hash
trm:term.trm:definition: A SHA-256 digest of the C14N form of a spec node subtree, stored in artifact mappings as the node-hash attribute. When a spec node is edited, its C14N form changes, producing a different hash. Comparing stored hashes against recomputed values detects spec-side drift.

trm:term.trm:name: Coverage
trm:term.trm:definition: The degree to which a spec node is implemented by its mapped artifact. Values: full (completely implemented), partial (some aspects missing), none (spec node has no implementation yet). Overimplementation (code without a spec mapping) is detected by diffing the artifact set against the spec node set.

pr:section.pr:title: Artifact Mapping and Drift Detection
pr:section.pr:shortdesc: How hashes enable bidirectional change detection between spec and code.
pr:section.pr:p: The artifact mapping layer is the most critical. Each mapping pins a spec node at a specific revision to a code artifact at a specific repository revision. Both sides carry content hashes.

When a spec node changes, its hash changes, and tooling flags artifact mappings that reference the old hash. When code changes, its content hash changes, and tooling flags spec nodes whose implementation has diverged. This is drift detection.

Coverage is explicit: full, partial, or none. Underimplementation is directly queryable from the mapping data. Overimplementation (code with no spec mapping) is detected by diffing the artifact set against the spec node set.

The spec-side hash is computed from the XML Canonical Form (C14N) of the node subtree. The canonicalization uses rewrite_prefixes mode so hashes are independent of whatever namespace prefixes the source file happened to use. This means renaming a prefix from pr: to prose: does not change the hash.
pr:section.pr:note: Artifact addressing uses file paths at a named repository revision. No AST addressing in the schema. The format is VCS-agnostic: it does not assume git, even if git is the first backend.

pr:section.pr:title: Artifact Hash Maintenance
pr:section.pr:shortdesc: How the CLI tool recomputes node hashes to keep artifact mappings current.
pr:section.pr:p: The artifact --fix-node-hash command recomputes node hashes in artifact mapping files. When spec prose is edited, the C14N hash of the affected nodes changes. This command brings the artifact layer back into sync without requiring manual hash calculation.

pr:steps.pr:step: Discover all specs reachable from the given path (file or directory), using the same index resolution as the validate command.
For each spec, find the current revision: the revision with the latest timestamp in the revision layer.
Collect all nodes (elements with @id) across all layer files in the spec.

For each spec-ref in the artifacts layer, check whether its revision attribute matches the current revision. Spec-refs pinned to older revisions are skipped: their hashes are historically correct and must not be updated.
For matching spec-refs, compute the C14N SHA-256 hash of the referenced node using rewrite_prefixes mode. Compare against the stored node-hash attribute.

Where hashes differ, update them using string replacement on the raw file text. This preserves the original XML formatting, comments, and attribute ordering. The tool does not rewrite or reformat the file.

pr:section.pr:note: Format-preserving writes are essential. Using an XML serializer would strip comments, reorder attributes, and normalize whitespace, creating noisy diffs that obscure the actual hash changes. String replacement on the raw text ensures only the hash values change.

pr:section.pr:title: Drift Detection Tooling
pr:section.pr:shortdesc: How the CLI tool detects spec-side and artifact-side drift without modifying files.
pr:section.pr:p: The artifact --drift command performs a read-only check across all artifact mappings in a spec. It detects both spec-side drift (node content changed, stored node hash is stale) and artifact-side drift (file content changed, stored range hash is stale).

pr:steps.pr:step: Discover all specs reachable from the given path, using the same index resolution as the validate command.
For each mapping's spec-ref, check whether the referenced node's current C14N hash matches the stored node-hash. Only check spec-refs pinned to the current revision.
For each mapping's artifact ranges, resolve the file path, extract the content region, and compare its hash against the stored range hash.
For artifact-side drift in git repositories, run git log repo-revision..HEAD -- path to identify the commits that caused the divergence.
Report results: a per-spec summary line, then per-mapping status. OK mappings get a single line. Drifted mappings expand with stored vs current hashes (truncated for readability) and causal commits.

pr:section.pr:note: The command exits with code 0 when no drift is found, and code 1 when any mapping has drifted. This enables use in CI pipelines and scripted checks.

org:reference.org:purpose: Reference material on artifact mapping, hashes, coverage, and drift detection mechanics.

org:task.org:purpose: The CLI tool recomputes C14N node hashes in artifact mappings, keeping them current after spec edits.
The CLI tool checks for spec-side and artifact-side drift across all artifact mappings, with git integration for causal commit identification.

rel:relation.rel:note: Drift detection depends on the artifact mapping structure with its dual hashes.
Hash maintenance depends on revision semantics to determine which spec-refs to update and which to skip.
Artifact mappings pin to revision names for temporal anchoring.
The hash maintenance tool depends on C14N for deterministic node hashing.
Hash maintenance is the mechanism that keeps artifact mappings in sync, preventing spec-side drift.
Drift detection implements the read-only checking described in the artifact drift reference material.
Drift detection reuses the same hash computation functions as hash maintenance (compute_node_hash, _compute_content_hash).
Drift detection depends on revision semantics to determine which spec-refs to check against the current revision.

art:mapping.art:note: check_drift: top-level drift checking across all mappings in a spec.
check_single_mapping: per-mapping spec-side and artifact-side drift detection.
ArtifactMapping/ArtifactRange structs (10-31), collect_artifact_mappings (38-79).
hash_file (222-225), extract_line_range (234-251), hash_line_range (258-265).
compute_node_hash() uses ET.canonicalize with rewrite_prefixes to produce a deterministic canonical form for hashing.
compute_node_hash() produces the SHA-256 digest of the C14N form, which is the node-hash stored in artifact mappings.
Hash comparison logic in _fix_node_hashes detects spec-side drift. The --drift command extends this with artifact-side detection and git commit attribution.
ArtifactMapping and ArtifactRange structs, collect_artifact_mappings public function.
MappingNames struct (82-99) and collect_mappings (101-118): XML name registration and mapping element collection.
parse_single_mapping (120-205): extracts spec-ref, artifact, ranges, coverage, and note from a single art:mapping element.
Schema header, annotation with LLM description, keyref declaration, and MappingType complexType define the structure of an artifact mapping: spec-ref, artifact, coverage, and note children.
SpecRefType defines the spec-ref element with node, revision, and node-hash attributes. The node-hash attribute carries the SHA-256 of the C14N form for spec-side drift detection.

ArtifactRefType (81-117) defines repo, repo-revision, path attributes and child range elements. RangeType (119-177) defines hash, line/column/byte-offset addressing, and XSD 1.1 assertions for paired attributes. Together they form the artifact-side of drift detection.
HashType simpleType constrains hash values to the algorithm:hexdigest pattern (e.g. sha256:abc123).
CoverageKind simpleType in artifact.xsd defines the full/partial/none enumeration for coverage declarations.
DriftStatus enum, MappingDrift struct, DriftReport struct: output types for drift detection.
check_drift: discovers specs, iterates mappings, assembles per-mapping drift status into DriftReport.
check_single_mapping: spec-side hash comparison, artifact-side file resolution and hash comparison.
collect_spec_node_ids, collect_nodes_recursive, hashes_match: helper functions for drift checking.
collect_artifact_mappings() discovers and parses artifact mappings from all spec files referenced by the index.
collect_artifact_mappings() collects revision info from spec-ref elements. Revision filtering is not yet implemented in the Rust version.
MappingNames struct and collect_mappings: XML name registration and mapping element collection.
parse_single_mapping: extracts spec-ref, artifact, ranges, coverage, and note from a single art:mapping element.
Revision check in _fix_node_hashes() skips spec-refs pinned to older revisions.
hash_file (222-225), extract_line_range (234-251), hash_line_range (258-265) compute SHA-256 of artifact content.
apply_range_changes (232-249) and apply_node_hash_changes (251-271) write updated hashes back to the XML source files.
fix_node_hashes() assembles the combined document, finds each referenced spec node, serializes via xot, applies C14N, computes SHA-256, and updates node-hash attributes in-place.
fix_artifact_hashes() discovers mappings, reads artifact files (with line-range addressing if specified), computes SHA-256, and updates hash attributes on art:range elements in-place.
replace_hash_in_mapping() performs anchored string replacement within a specific mapping block, using mapping ID and optional start-line/end-line attributes to locate the correct element.
cmd_fix_hashes() dispatches --fix-node-hash and --fix-artifact-hash CLI flags to the fix module and prints the fix report.
find_repo_root() walks up from spec_dir looking for .git. resolve_artifact_path() resolves artifact paths relative to repo root or by walking up from spec_dir. Used by both fix and drift modules.
find_index_files() discovers specs from the given path.
Spec-side check in _check_drift() compares C14N node hash against stored node-hash.
Artifact-side check in _check_drift() resolves file path, extracts content region, compares hash.
check_single_mapping (90-164) checks both spec and artifact hashes. Git log attribution for causal commits is not yet ported to Rust.
cmd_drift() formats and prints per-mapping drift status (OK, SPEC DRIFTED, ARTIFACT DRIFTED, UNAVAILABLE).

rel:relation.rel:note: Step: discover all specs from path.
Step: find current revision by latest timestamp.
Step: collect all elements with @id.
Step: check spec-ref revision matches current.
Step: compute C14N hash and compare.
Step: update hashes via string replacement.
Step: discover all specs from path.
Step: check spec-side C14N hash against stored node-hash.
Step: resolve artifact path, extract content, compare hash.
Step: run git log for causal commit identification.
Step: report per-spec summary and per-mapping status.

spec:clayers.llm:schema: The artifact schema maps spec nodes to code artifacts for traceability and drift detection. Each <mapping> contains a spec-ref (node ID + revision + C14N hash of the node) and an optional artifact (repo + repo-revision + file path) with zero or more child <range> elements. Each range carries its own content hash and optional addressing (line range, byte offsets, line+column). Zero ranges means pointer-only reference. Coverage is explicit: full, partial, or none. When either side's hash changes, tooling detects drift.

References a spec node at a specific revision. The node attribute is the node ID, revision is the revision name, and node-hash is the SHA-256 of the C14N form of the node subtree. The hash enables drift detection: if the node content changes, the hash changes.

References a code artifact. The repo attribute identifies the repository, repo-revision pins a specific commit/tag, path is the file path relative to the repo root. Contains zero or more child <range> elements for content hashing. Zero ranges means a pointer-only reference without content tracking. VCS-agnostic: does not assume git.

A content region within an artifact file. The hash attribute is the SHA-256 of the selected content. Addressing is optional: no addressing attributes means entire file; start-line/end-line selects a line range (1-based inclusive); start-col/end-col adds column precision (requires all four line+col attrs); start-offset/end-offset selects a byte range. Line+col and byte offsets may coexist for redundancy.
spec:clayers.llm:node: Reference material on how hashes enable bidirectional drift detection between spec and code. Important for understanding the artifact mapping layer's value proposition.

The artifact --fix-node-hash command recomputes C14N node hashes in artifact mapping files. It finds the current revision (latest by timestamp), skips spec-refs pinned to older revisions, computes SHA-256 of the C14N form with rewrite_prefixes, and updates hashes via string replacement on raw file text to preserve formatting. This maps directly to the artifact command in clayers-cli.

The artifact --drift command performs a read-only check of all artifact mappings in a spec. For each mapping it checks both spec-side drift (recomputing C14N node hash vs stored node-hash) and artifact-side drift (recomputing content hash of file ranges vs stored range hash). For artifact-side drift in git repos, it runs git log to identify causal commits. Output is terse and structured for LLM consumption: OK mappings get one line, drifted mappings expand with truncated hashes and commit summaries. Exit code 0 means no drift, 1 means drift found.

trm:term.trm:name: Artifact Coverage
trm:term.trm:definition: The degree to which code and spec mutually reference each other through artifact mappings, measured bidirectionally. Code coverage measures which lines in mapped files are covered by at least one artifact range. Spec coverage measures which content nodes (terms, sections) have at least one artifact mapping, either directly or by parent inheritance. Nodes declared exempt via coverage exemption are excluded from the unmapped count. Full coverage means every non-whitespace code fragment has a mapping and every non-exempt spec node is reachable from at least one mapping.

trm:term.trm:name: Coverage Exemption
trm:term.trm:definition: A declaration that a spec node does not require artifact coverage. Expressed as an art:exempt element with a required node attribute pointing to the exempt node's ID and optional inline text explaining the reason. Exemptions are intended for documentation-only nodes (vocabulary terms, conceptual prose, navigation structures like reading maps) that describe the specification format itself and have no corresponding code artifact. Exempt nodes are reported separately in coverage analysis, distinct from both mapped and unmapped nodes.

pr:section.pr:title: Coverage Analysis Tooling
pr:section.pr:shortdesc: How the CLI tool measures bidirectional coverage between spec nodes and code artifacts.
pr:section.pr:p: The artifact --coverage command analyzes artifact coverage across all artifact mappings in a spec. It produces two reports: code coverage (which lines in mapped files are not covered by any mapping range) and spec coverage (which content nodes have no artifact mapping).

Code coverage works at line granularity. For each mapped file, the tool builds a set of covered line numbers from all artifact ranges, then identifies contiguous uncovered fragments. Whitespace-only fragments (blank lines, indentation) are silently filtered because they carry no semantic content. Whole-file mappings (no start-line/end-line) cover 100% of the file. Byte-offset-only ranges produce a warning because line-based coverage cannot be computed.

Spec coverage walks all content nodes (terms, sections) and checks whether each has a direct artifact mapping or inherits coverage from a parent node. Nodes declared as exempt via art:exempt elements are reported in a separate category, distinct from both mapped and unmapped nodes. Elements from the artifact namespace (art:mapping, art:exempt) are excluded from the report because they are metadata, not content. The --code-path option filters code coverage to files matching a specific path substring.

The command exits with code 0 when all code fragments are covered and all non-exempt spec nodes are mapped (full coverage), and code 1 when any gaps exist. This enables use in CI pipelines to enforce coverage requirements.

org:task.org:purpose: The CLI tool measures bidirectional artifact coverage, identifying uncovered code fragments and unmapped spec nodes.

rel:relation.rel:note: Coverage tooling depends on the artifact coverage concept for its semantic foundation.
Coverage analysis builds on the artifact mapping structure that drift detection also uses.
Coverage analysis uses the coverage enumeration (full, partial, none) from the artifact schema.
Coverage analysis uses exemption declarations to separate exempt nodes from truly unmapped ones.
Coverage exemptions modify the artifact coverage metric by excluding specific nodes from the unmapped count.

art:mapping.art:note: The art:exempt element declaration in artifact.xsd implements the coverage exemption concept as an XML schema element.
_collect_exempt_nodes() collects art:exempt declarations from spec files and returns a dict of node_id to reason text.
CoverageStrength enum, classify_strength, SpecCoverage enum, CoverageReport, FileCoverage, CoveredRange, UncoveredRange, ArtifactCoverage structs.
analyze_coverage: public entry point that discovers mappings, builds coverage maps, and produces the full CoverageReport.
The coverage analysis functions implement the artifact coverage concept: bidirectional measurement of code-to-spec and spec-to-code coverage through artifact mappings.

spec:clayers.llm:node: The artifact --coverage command measures bidirectional coverage between spec and code. Code coverage reports uncovered line fragments in mapped files, filtering whitespace-only gaps. Spec coverage reports unmapped content nodes, applying parent inheritance. The --code-path option filters to specific files. Exit code 0 means full coverage, 1 means gaps exist. Designed for both human consumption (Rich-formatted) and CI pipelines.

Artifact coverage measures how completely code and spec reference each other through artifact mappings. It is bidirectional: code coverage checks which lines are covered by mapping ranges, spec coverage checks which nodes have mappings (directly or by parent inheritance). Nodes declared exempt via art:exempt are excluded from the unmapped count.

Coverage exemption (art:exempt) declares a spec node as not needing artifact coverage. Used for documentation-only nodes: vocabulary terms, conceptual prose, navigation structures like reading maps. The element carries a required node attribute (the exempt node's ID) and optional inline text explaining why. _collect_exempt_nodes() in clayers-cli collects these declarations; _report_spec_coverage() reports them in a separate category from mapped and unmapped nodes. Exempt nodes do not count against coverage metrics.

trm:term.trm:name: Coverage Strength
trm:term.trm:definition: A categorical metric describing how precisely an artifact mapping targets code. Strength is determined by the line span of the broadest range in a mapping: precise (1-30 lines) targets a single function, moderate (31-100 lines) covers a cluster of related functions, broad (101+ lines or whole-file) spans a large section. Narrower mappings are stronger because they provide more precise traceability between spec nodes and code.

pr:section.pr:title: Coverage Strength Analysis
pr:section.pr:shortdesc: How the CLI tool measures and reports the precision of artifact mappings.
pr:section.pr:p: Coverage strength measures how precisely each artifact mapping targets code. A mapping that covers 11 lines (a single function) provides stronger traceability than one covering 200 lines (a large module section). The strength metric encourages driving mappings toward precise, function-level granularity.
Strength is determined by the line span of the broadest range in a mapping. Three tiers are defined:

pr:ul.pr:li: Precise (1-30 lines): targets a single function or definition. Provides the strongest traceability.
Moderate (31-100 lines): covers a cluster of related functions. Acceptable but could be tightened.
Broad (101+ lines or whole-file): spans a large section or entire file. Should be split into targeted mappings where possible.

pr:section.pr:p: The artifact --coverage command annotates each COVERED range with its strength label and line count (e.g., "COVERED 263-273 (map-term-c14n) [precise, 11 lines]"). A per-file summary shows the distribution across tiers. In spec coverage, each covered node is annotated with the strength of its mapping.
pr:section.pr:note: Whole-file mappings (no start-line/end-line) are always classified as broad regardless of file size, because they lack the precision to trace specific code to specific spec nodes.

org:task.org:purpose: The CLI tool classifies artifact mapping ranges by line span into precision tiers and reports per-file strength distribution.

rel:relation.rel:note: The strength analysis feature depends on the coverage strength concept for its tier definitions.
Strength analysis extends the coverage analysis output with per-range and per-file strength annotations.

art:mapping.art:note: _is_whitespace_only(), PRECISE_MAX, MODERATE_MAX, _strength_label() (645-661) define the metric. _mapping_strength() (1145-1172) computes per-mapping strength from range spans. The modified _report_code_coverage and _report_spec_coverage annotate output with strength labels.
PRECISE_MAX, MODERATE_MAX constants and _strength_label() implement the three-tier categorization defined by the coverage strength term.

spec:clayers.llm:node: Coverage strength analysis classifies artifact mapping ranges into three precision tiers based on line span: precise (1-30 lines), moderate (31-100 lines), broad (101+ lines or whole-file). The coverage command annotates each COVERED range with its tier and line count, and adds a per-file summary showing tier distribution. Whole-file mappings are always broad. The metric drives mappings toward function-level granularity for stronger traceability.

Coverage strength is a categorical metric (precise/moderate/broad) based on the line span of an artifact mapping's broadest range. Precise maps target single functions, moderate covers function clusters, broad spans large sections. Narrower is better for traceability.

art:mapping.art:note: collect_node_ids, count_non_whitespace_lines, FileRangeEntry struct, build_coverage_maps: constructs per-file line-to-mapping coverage maps.
compute_single_file_coverage (257-310): per-file coverage computation from line-to-mapping maps.
compute_file_coverages (313-366) and find_uncovered_ranges (369-405): file iteration and uncovered range detection.
FileCoverage, CoveredRange, UncoveredRange, ArtifactCoverage struct definitions for code→spec coverage reporting.
cmd_connectivity() CLI command: runs connectivity analysis and prints graph metrics, components, hubs, bridges, cycles.
cmd_query() CLI command: executes XPath queries against the combined spec document with count, text, and XML output modes.
cmd_artifact() coverage display and default artifact listing mode. Shows per-mapping strength, unmapped nodes, and per-file code coverage.
CLI structure: Cli struct, Command enum with all subcommands (validate, artifact, connectivity, query), cli_main() entry point, run() dispatcher.
cmd_drift() CLI command: runs drift detection and prints per-mapping status with stored vs current hashes and causal commits.
ContentHash struct, all methods (from_bytes, from_canonical, to_hex, to_prefixed), and trait implementations (Display, Debug, FromStr).
Full C14N module: CanonicalizationMode enum (6 variants), canonicalize(), canonicalize_str(), canonicalize_and_hash() bridge functions.
ConnectivityReport, HubNode, BridgeNode, Cycle struct definitions for graph metric output.
build_graph and weakly_connected_components: adjacency list construction and component detection.
find_isolated_nodes (338-359) and find_hub_nodes (361-384): degree-based node analysis.
find_bridge_nodes (386-466): betweenness centrality bridge node detection.
find_cycles: DFS-based cycle detection with acyclic violation checking.
find_index_files, discover_spec_files, collect_file_refs: index discovery and spec file manifest reading.
find_xml_files, walkdir, is_index_file, resolve_index: XML file discovery and index detection helpers.
FixResult (10-14) and FixReport (18-23) struct definitions.
RangeChange (194-200), NodeHashChange (202-206), find_node_by_id (210-230), apply_range_changes (232-249), apply_node_hash_changes (251-271).
QueryResult and QueryMode enums, execute_query() public entry point.
XPath evaluation functions: evaluate_xpath_count, evaluate_xpath_text, evaluate_xpath_xml, collect_all_text, collect_text_recursive, resolve_steps.
ParsedStep (200-205) and ResolvedStep (207-213) structs, plus parse entry point constants.
parse_xpath_steps (215-234), split_xpath_path, parse_single_step, parse_predicate: XPath parsing implementation.
Node matching: find_nodes_by_steps tree-walking, matches_step element/attribute/predicate matching.
assemble_combined: creates combined document by unwrapping layer file roots into cmb:spec wrapper.
extract_inner_xml, assemble_combined_string, _api module: XML content extraction and string assembly helpers.
ValidationResult and ValidationError structs, imports.
check_well_formed, check_id_uniqueness, collect_ids: ID uniqueness enforcement across all layer files.
check_references and collect_all_ids: keyref validation of cross-layer node references.
check_relation_refs: validates that relation from/to attributes reference existing node IDs.
ContentElement/Keyref structs, discover_acyclic_types, collect_acyclic_types: XSD scanning for acyclic relation type annotations.
has_acyclic_true, discover_content_elements, discover_from_xsd: top-level content element discovery functions.
collect_content_elements, has_content_element_annotation: recursive XSD element collection with appinfo annotation checking.
FixNodeHashReport struct definition.
Basic replace_hash_in_mapping tests: node-hash replacement, range with line anchor, whole-file mapping, non-matching mapping.
Advanced replace_hash_in_mapping tests: missing attribute, first range selection, preserves surrounding content.
Synthetic spec test helpers and basic fix_artifact_hashes integration tests.
Remaining synthetic test cases: idempotent, line ranges, skips missing files.
Basic fix_node_hashes tests: updates hash, idempotent, deterministic, different content.
Shipped spec integration tests: fix_node_hashes on real spec, idempotent check, copy_dir_all helper.
Test module for hash.rs: ContentHash round-trip, to_prefixed format, from_canonical determinism, Display/FromStr round-trip.
Test module for assembly.rs: combined document assembly from shipped spec, namespace verification, multi-layer content merging.
Test module for namespace.rs: all 12 URN constants are distinct, prefix map covers all layers.
Test module for c14n.rs: canonicalization determinism, inclusive vs exclusive mode differences, canonicalize_and_hash integration.
Unit tests: strength classification, whitespace filtering, uncovered range detection, single file coverage computation.
build_coverage_maps tests: path filtering, whole-file vs ranged mappings.
Shipped spec integration tests for coverage analysis.
Test module for query.rs: XPath count/text/XML queries against shipped spec, namespace prefix registration, term and relation queries.
Imports, collect_text_content helper, inter-function gaps, and test module for artifact.rs: mapping collection from shipped spec, hash consistency, line-range extraction, hash differentiation.
Test module for validate.rs: ID uniqueness detection, valid spec passes, shipped spec validation, malformed XML detection.
Test module for drift.rs: no-drift and drift-detected paths, shipped spec drift check.
Test module for schema.rs: content-element discovery, acyclic type discovery from shipped schemas.
Test module for connectivity.rs: graph metrics, connected components, isolated nodes, hub identification, cycle detection.
Test module for discovery.rs: file discovery from shipped spec, index resolution, path existence verification.
Error enum for clayers-xml: XmlParse, Canonicalization, HashFormat variants via thiserror, plus Display verification tests.
CoverageKind partial/none enumerations and HashType simpleType.
cmd_artifact() function signature and dispatch to fix_hashes/drift.
find_schema_dir() helper: walks up from spec_dir to find schemas/ directory.
Single-line gaps between schema type definitions (closing/opening tags).
